## [06:04 - 09:07]

Now that could mean that you do it for someone else. Doesn't mean you have to do this for your own business. Doesn't mean you have to be selling a product in order to learn Google AdWords. But certainly, going and doing this a little bit, riding a bike, you can't learn from reading the book. You have to go do it. And that's what's going to really improve your learning. So what is Google AdWords? Well, it's been called the greatest direct response machine ever invented. Direct response, I'm a direct response guy. I'm not a branding marketing guy. I care about the result of the dollar spent. So for every dollar I tip in, I want to get three back or seven or 28. Whatever it is, but I want to get a positive return on that dollar that I'm tipping in. I want to get more than a dollar back, in other words. It's also called the traffic store. And Ryan Dice has called it this many a time. It's where people go. And so we want to go to where the people are. They're looking for websites. Let's put our website there in front of them so that they can find us. And if you haven't been convinced yet why you might want to use it, well, it's very, very fast to get results. You may have heard of SEO, search engine optimization, whereby you put content on your website and you tweak things and you do it in a certain way and you pay somebody money to do it for you. And over time, the idea being that your website will rank higher in the Google search engine. So for various keywords, your website will go higher and higher and higher up the page, heading towards that mythical number one spot. Everybody wants to be number one. That can take months. If your SEO guys are good, it might take weeks. But it often takes months. And for a competitive keyword, for a keyword that lots of other businesses want to be number one for, it might take six to 12 months. Or even more. With AdWords, you can be showing ads on the first page within half an hour. Now, you have to pay for those clicks, but you can at least get an ad up there and test, test your assumption. Is this a keyword I should be buying? Is this something I should be spending money on? Should I be showing on the first page? Should I be showing in Austin, as well as New York, as well as London? Or is it only profitable in Austin? Or only profitable Monday to Friday? That's the stuff that we're going to be able to do once we've got our AdWords account running. It's also very scalable. Once you find bits that are winning, you can scale that up. And we'll talk about that as we go through. So it's very predictable because it's measurable and scalable. It's predictable. It's a vending machine, not a slot machine. As Dean Jackson, one of the greatest marketers out there, often says, you want to build a vending machine, not a slot machine. The slot machine is a gamble. It's a guess. A vending machine is incredibly predictable. You know, I put a dollar in, I press the button and a Canada Coke falls out the bottom. I know exactly what I'm going to get from my dollar. That's what you're building by building a really, really good AdWords account. You're, I won't, I won't call it a money machine because things can change.

## [09:07 - 11:46]

Competitors can bid more. Lots of different things can change. But you're building a very predictable marketing machine. And your ads can be all over the place. We're mainly going to focus, or actually we're only going to focus, in this course on the search side of Google AdWords. The left-hand side there where everybody's familiar with what Google looks like and the four ads at the top of the page. There's also ads at the bottom and obviously ads on page two. It looks slightly different on a mobile device. But there is so much more to Google AdWords. There's this thing called the display network whereby you can show ads like you see down here on the right-hand side, ads on Oprah.com or on CNN or ESPN, or one of two million plus websites, most of which you or I have never heard of, covering all manner of topics and discussions. And you can put your ads on any of those. Yes, you have to pay if somebody clicks on your ad, but to put your ad there is free. For the 99% of people that see it on those display sites, it's free branding. And you just pay when someone clicks. You can also put ads on YouTube. The wonderful Tom Breeze in the UK is the expert on YouTube advertising and video advertising. You can put ads inside Gmail, inside apps on people's phones. There is almost no limit. Obviously, the other huge traffic source is Facebook, and that's what Molly is talking about in the rest of this program. So I'm going to talk about AdWords and specifically the search side of AdWords. I'm going to focus on what we like to call the bottom of the funnel. So you've probably heard Molly talk about this before, Tofu, MoFu and BoFu, top of the funnel, middle of the funnel, bottom of the funnel. I'm going to focus on the bottom of the funnel. In other words, after people have done all their research, the bit where they get the wallet out and they're ready to buy, that's going to be the most profitable place for you to run ads, the most profitable keywords for you to bid on, where they are most likely to have a positive return on investment. So we're not trying to do branding here. We're not trying to get people to remember our names. We're trying to show an ad to people that are ready to go, hot the trot, pulling the credit card out by showing an ad to those people only to start with. We've got our best chance of increasing our return on investment. So there you go, quick introduction, quick 10 minutes of AdWords. What it is, how it can help your business. I would love it if you stick with us for the rest of the modules and come and understand how AdWords can work for your business. I'll see you in the next module.
